title: Credat Judaeus Apella
content:
آ آ آ آ Dear Bell, I enclose what you ask in a letter,
آ آ آ آ A short rhyme at random, no more and no less,
آ آ آ آ And you may insert it, for want of a better,
آ آ آ آ Or leave it, it doesnï؟½t much matter, I guess;
آ آ آ آ And as for a tip, why, there isnï؟½t much in it,
آ آ آ آ I may hit the right nail, but first, I declare,
آ آ آ آ I havenï؟½t a notion whatï؟½s going to win it
آ آ آ آ (The Champion, I mean), and whatï؟½s more, I donï؟½t care.
آ آ آ آ Imprimis, thereï؟½s Cowra, few nags can go quicker
آ آ آ آ Than she can, and Smith takes his oath she can fly;
آ آ آ آ While Brown, Jones, and Robinson swear sheï؟½s a sticker,
آ آ آ آ But ï؟½credat Judaeus Apellaï؟½, say I.

آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s old Volunteer, Iï؟½d be sorry to sneer
آ آ آ آ At his chance; heï؟½ll be there, if he goes at the rate
آ آ آ آ He went at last year, when a customer queer,
آ آ آ آ Johnny Higgerson, fancied him lockï؟½d in the straight;
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve heard that the old horse has never been fitter,
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve heard all performances past heï؟½ll outvie;
آ آ آ آ He may gallop a docker, and finish a splitter,
آ آ آ آ But ï؟½credat Judaeus Apellaï؟½, say I.

آ آ آ آ I know what they say, sir, ï؟½The Hookï؟½ he can stay, sir,
آ آ آ آ And stick to his work like a sleuth-hound or beagle;
آ آ آ آ He stays ï؟½with a hookï؟½, and he sticks in the clay, sir;
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½d rather, for choice, pop my money on Seagull;
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½m told that the Sydney division will rue, sir,
آ آ آ آ Their rashness in front of the stand when they spy,
آ آ آ آ With a clear lead, the white jacket spotted with blue, sir,
آ آ آ آ But ï؟½credat Judaeus Apellaï؟½, say I.

آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s The Barb, you may talk of your flyers and stayers,
آ آ آ آ All bosh, when he strips you can see his eye range
آ آ آ آ Round his rivals, with much the same look as Tom Sayers
آ آ آ آ Once wore when he faced the big novice, Bill Bainge.
آ آ آ آ Like Stow, at our hustings, confronting the hisses
آ آ آ آ Of roughs, with his queer Mephistophelesï؟½ smile;
آ آ آ آ Like Baker, or Bakerï؟½s more wonderful Mrs.,
آ آ آ آ The terror of blacks at the source of the Nile;
آ آ آ آ Like Triton ï؟½mid minnows; like hawk among chickens;
آ آ آ آ Like, anything better than everything else:
آ آ آ آ He stands at the post. Now theyï؟½re off! the plot thickens!
آ آ آ آ Quoth Stanley to Davis, ï؟½How is your pulse?ï؟½
آ آ آ آ He skims oï؟½er the smooth turf, he scuds through the mire,
آ آ آ آ He waits with them, passes them, bids them good-bye!
آ آ آ آ Two miles and three-quarters, cries Filgate, ï؟½Heï؟½ll tire.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Oh! ï؟½credat Judaeus Apellaï؟½, say I.

آ آ آ آ Lest my tale should come true, let me give you fair warning,
آ آ آ آ You may ï؟½shoutï؟½ some cheroots, if you like, no champagne
آ آ آ آ For this child, ï؟½Oh! think of my head in the morning,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Old chap, you donï؟½t get me on that lay again.
آ آ آ آ The last time those games I lookï؟½d likely to try on,
آ آ آ آ Says Bradshawe, ï؟½Youï؟½ll feel very sheepish and shy
آ آ آ آ When you are haulï؟½d up and cautionï؟½d by D--g--y and L--n,ï؟½
آ آ آ آ Oh! ï؟½credat Judaeus Apellaï؟½, say I.

آ آ آ آ This writing bad verses is very fatiguing,
آ آ آ آ The brain and the liver against it combine,
آ آ آ آ And nerves with digestion in concert are leaguing,
آ آ آ آ To punish excess in the pen and ink line;
آ آ آ آ Already I feel just as if Iï؟½d been rowing
آ آ آ آ Hard all, on a supper of onions and tripe
آ آ آ آ (A thing I abhor), but my steam Iï؟½ve done blowing,
آ آ آ آ I am, my dear bell, yours truly, ï؟½The Pipeï؟½.

آ آ آ آ P.S. Tell J. P., if he fancies a good ï؟½un,
آ آ آ آ That old chestnut pony of mine is for sale.
آ آ آ آ N.B. His forelegs are uncommonly wooden,
آ آ آ آ I fancy the near oneï؟½s beginning to fail,
آ آ آ آ And why shouldnï؟½t I do as W--n does oft,
آ آ آ آ And swear that a cripple is sound, on the Bible,
آ آ آ آ Hold hard! though the man I allude to is soft,
آ آ آ آ Heï؟½s game to go in for an action of libel.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Adam Lindsay Gordon represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Adam Lindsay Gordon's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
